title: Sleep Cycle Calculator
description: Industrial-grade tool to optimize sleep-wake timing based on circadian rhythm, sleep debt, and recovery efficiency. Aligns with ISO 10075 (ergonomic principles), Lean (waste of fatigue), and Six Sigma (variation reduction in rest).

inputs.label: Desired wake-up time (24h)
inputs.businessContext: Target end of sleep cycle; used to back-calculate optimal bedtimes.

businessContext_i18n.en: Target end of sleep cycle; used to back-calculate optimal bedtimes.

inputs.label: Accumulated sleep debt (hours)
inputs.businessContext: Cumulative deficit from prior nights; drives recovery need per sleep science.

businessContext_i18n.en: Cumulative deficit from prior nights; drives recovery need per sleep science.

inputs.label: Average sleep cycle length
inputs.businessContext: Typical NREM/REM cycle duration; 90 min is standard, adjustable per individual.

businessContext_i18n.en: Typical NREM/REM cycle duration; 90 min is standard, adjustable per individual.

inputs.label: Estimated time to fall asleep
inputs.businessContext: Sleep onset latency; affects actual sleep start time.

businessContext_i18n.en: Sleep onset latency; affects actual sleep start time.

inputs.label: Recovery efficiency factor
inputs.businessContext: Adjusts sleep debt repayment rate (1.0 = normal, <1.0 = impaired recovery, >1.0 = enhanced).

businessContext_i18n.en: Adjusts sleep debt repayment rate (1.0 = normal, <1.0 = impaired recovery, >1.0 = enhanced).

inputs.label: Shift worker?
inputs.businessContext: Flag to apply circadian disruption penalty per ISO 10075-2.

businessContext_i18n.en: Flag to apply circadian disruption penalty per ISO 10075-2.

rules.message: Wake time outside typical circadian window (4:00–12:00). Results may be less accurate.
Cycle length outside physiological range (60–120 min).

thresholds.message: Sleep debt exceeds 4 hours; risk of performance degradation (Six Sigma defect).
Recovery efficiency critically low; consider medical evaluation.

formulas.name: Actual sleep start time
formulas.description: Wake time minus total sleep duration including fall-asleep latency.

formulas.name: Total sleep minutes needed
formulas.description: Base sleep need (7.5h = 5 cycles) plus debt repayment adjusted by efficiency.

formulas.name: Optimal bedtime (back-calculated)
formulas.description: Wake time minus total sleep minutes minus fall-asleep latency.

formulas.name: Circadian disruption penalty
formulas.description: If shift worker, adds 0.5h to sleep debt equivalent.

formulas.name: Number of complete sleep cycles
formulas.description: Total sleep minutes divided by cycle length, rounded down.

formulas.name: Sleep quality index (0–100)
formulas.description: Composite score based on cycle count, debt, and efficiency.

primary.label: Optimal bedtime
primary.description: Recommended time to go to bed to wake up at desired time with minimal sleep inertia.

hiddenLossDrivers.description: Caffeine consumed within 6h of bedtime can increase sleep onset latency by 30–60 min, not modeled here.
Blue light suppresses melatonin; can reduce recovery efficiency by up to 20%.
Undiagnosed sleep apnea can reduce recovery efficiency by 40% or more.

suggestedActions.description: Maintain same wake time every day (including weekends) to stabilize circadian rhythm.
If adjusted sleep debt > 2h, add one extra sleep cycle (90 min) for 2–3 nights.
Begin wind-down 30 min before optimal bedtime: dim lights, no screens, cool room (18–20°C).
Use blackout curtains and scheduled naps (20 min) to offset circadian penalty.
